text: >This entire post reads like a long apology for a system that kills people quietly and profitably, not an actual defense of material reality. My post isn't to apologize for Brian Thompson. My post is to debunk for him, since he's no longer around to debunk the accusations himself, with said accusations being either disproven already or unconfirmed. >You can list every clerical reason a claim might be denied, but none of that changes the basic fact that the U.S. health insurance industry exists to extract profit- not to deliver care. And when profit and human life conflict, capitalism chooses profit every single time. That’s not conspiracy; that’s the incentive structure. That’s Marx 101. Who says insurance giants can't do both? They generate profits, yes, but UnitedHealthcare helps deliver care at the same time by covering hundreds of millions of claims annually. They wouldn't have covered so many under Brian Thompson's watch if Brian Thompson was this profit addict hyper-obsessed with profiting that you're making him out to be. >The reason people reacted to Thompson’s death the way they did isn’t because of “rumors” or “lies”, it’s because every single one of us has seen or experienced how deadly this system is. You can't hand-wave that material reality away by pointing out that some claims were coded wrong. The main reason UnitedHealthcare denies claims is either due to clerical reasons, or to prevent fraud, waste, and abuse, not because "profit and human life conflict". If you disagree on this, then give a specific concrete example of UnitedHealthcare "killing" someone on Brian Thompson's watch for that specific reason — "because profit and human life conflicted" — and not for any of the legitimate reasons I've listed. >pressuring providers to choose cheaper options even when clinically inappropriate. Let's see a specific concrete example of this as well — of UnitedHealthcare pressuring a provider on Brian Thompson's watch to choose a "clinically inappropriate cheaper option". >delaying care, UnitedHealthcare gave legitimate reasons there may sometimes be delays in care. It's because some claims genuinely need additional review before they can be approved. I linked in my post their article where they explain this, saying, and I quote: * While it may seem like claims denials are prevalent, in reality, the vast majority of Americans’ experience is simple and straightforward. * When you ask Americans what’s important to them when it comes to health care, having access to the care they need and being able to afford that care are usually at the top of the list. So it’s no wonder that having a claim denied is something no one wants to go through. Fortunately, most Americans never do. * Why do health insurance companies deny claims or flag them for further review? * At UnitedHealthcare, we approve and pay 90% of claims shortly after they’re submitted. The remaining 10% go through an additional review process. * Some of the main reasons a claim may be reviewed further after submission include: * Eligibility: The most common reason a claim might fall into this category is because there’s a possibility the patient is not actually a UnitedHealthcare member. This situation most often occurs when someone has recently switched to a new plan but didn’t share their current health plan ID card with their provider. The review process for these claims is typically simple and straightforward. Once we’ve confirmed the patient is enrolled in one of our plans, we approve and process the claim. * Duplicates: Occasionally providers submit multiple claims for the same treatment or service. In these cases, we collaborate with the physician or hospital to resolve these duplicate submissions, ensuring we are only reviewing one claim per treatment or service. * Documentation and program integrity: Claims fall into this category because the provider didn’t submit all the information necessary to review the claim. In these cases, we follow up with the care provider to notify them of the issue and work to resolve it. * Coverage: We review claims in this category to confirm that the plan sponsor – the employer, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services or a state government – included coverage for that particular service or procedure when designing their health plan. * Clinical: Claims in this category are reviewed to ensure the care provided aligns with evidence-based clinical guidelines. While we can’t control doctors’ treatment decisions, we believe we have an important role in ensuring our members receive safe, appropriate and high-quality care. When the treatment our members receive does not adhere to widely accepted clinical standards, we do not approve payment to the care provider. It should be noted that only about 0.5% of claims fall into this category. >Even Medicare Advantage, run by these same corporations, has been repeatedly caught denying medically necessary care and upcoding patients to overbill taxpayers. This is a complete lie. UnitedHealthcare was found in court to have done nothing of the sort: [<URL> >The reason people reacted to Thompson’s death the way they did isn’t because of “rumors” or “lies”, it’s because every single one of us has seen or experienced how deadly this system is. You can't hand-wave that material reality away by pointing out that some claims were coded wrong. >If you want fewer tragedies- on any side, the solution isn’t to moralize at angry people online. It’s to confront the root cause: healthcare should never have been turned into a commodity. This entire conversation only exists because we allow corporations to decide who lives and who dies based on profit margins. That’s the violence worth abolishing. People care about whether their health insurance claims get approved or not, not about whether healthcare is a commodity or not. When their claims get denied due to clerical reasons or the other legitimate reasons listed in my post, but they're repeatedly lied to about why such denials occurred — that it's simply because UnitedHealthcare is evil and greedy and cares about profits and nothing else, that it's because UnitedHealthcare puts AI with 90% error rates in charge of denying claims, etc. — many of those people become radicalized into vehemently hating UnitedHealthcare, hence all this hatred towards them and cheering and celebrating of Brian Thompson's assassination, even though chances are they could've gotten their claims approved simply by re-reviewing and correcting said claims to make sure everything was clerically correct, that any needed documentation and evidence of medical necessity was included, that the insured's insurance in question was up to date, that there was no fraud in the claim, etc. So yes, I agree with you that the root cause needs to be confronted, and I agree with you that there are those with bad experiences involving UnitedHealthcare because people after enough time will have bad experiences involving any health insurer whether it's UnitedHealthcare or the insurance my family uses which is 1199SEIU, but what I'm saying is that people have become too quick to blame these root causes and bad experiences involving UnitedHealthcare on those unconfirmed/disproven rumors/accusations I mentioned. This quickness costed Brian Thompson his life and is threatening to possibly inspire future copycat assassinations.
